Sample Digest · Monday 27 April 2026

A recent Dermatology digest,
as sent to subscribers

This is the real Monday digest from Monday 27 April 2026. Showing the first 8 of 15 articles from that week — every new paper from the top journals in Dermatology.

15articles that week
5journals covered
£0for your first 4 weeks
Note: Summaries are AI-generated from publicly available PubMed abstracts. Always verify against the source article before clinical use.
JAMA dermatology

Stapokibart for the Treatment of Refractory Palmoplantar Pustulosis

Liu X, Wu Y, Liu Z et al. · 2026 Apr 22
Study Type: Cannot be determined - abstract text not provided.
Key Question: Cannot be determined without the abstract content.
Key Findings:
  • Abstract text is missing, preventing extraction of study findings
  • Only title indicates this relates to stapokibart treatment for refractory palmoplantar pustulosis
Clinical Relevance: Cannot assess clinical relevance without access to the study methodology, results, and conclusions from the abstract.
Limitations: Cannot evaluate study limitations as the abstract content is not available for review. Note: To provide a meaningful clinical summary, the full abstract text is required. The title suggests this study examines stapokibart (likely a novel therapeutic agent) for treating refractory palmoplantar pustulosis, a challenging inflammatory skin condition affecting palms and soles that often responds poorly to conventional therapies.
PubMed →DOI →
JAMA dermatology

Diagnostic Modalities and Nodal Staging in High-Risk Cutaneous Squamous Cell Carcinoma

Ferrándiz-Pulido C, Gómez-Tomás Á, Siurana S et al. · 2026 Apr 22
Study Type: Prospective multicenter diagnostic study
Key Question: How do physical examination, ultrasonography, and CT compare for detecting nodal metastases at baseline staging in high-risk cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma?
Key Findings:
  • Ultrasonography showed highest sensitivity (63.6%) followed by CT (54.5%) and physical examination (8.3%), with comparable high specificities (>95%) for ultrasound and CT
  • In immunocompetent patients, both ultrasonography and CT achieved 100% sensitivity with excellent diagnostic performance (AUROC 0.98)
  • In immunosuppressed patients, sensitivity dropped markedly for both modalities (20.0% ultrasound, 16.7% CT) with poor overall performance (AUROC ~0.55)
Clinical Relevance: These findings support routine imaging (ultrasonography or CT) over clinical examination alone for baseline nodal staging in high-risk cSCC, particularly relevant given the NHS emphasis on early detection and appropriate staging pathways.
Limitations: Poor diagnostic performance in immunosuppressed patients limits the generalisability of findings to this high-risk subgroup who comprise a significant proportion of advanced cSCC cases.
PubMed →DOI →
Journal of the American Academy of Dermatology

Hydroxychloroquine Use and 5-Year Cardiometabolic Profile and Cardiovascular Risk in Isolated Discoid Lupus Erythematosus: A Retrospective Cohort Study

Srikumar A, Yuvchenko O, Kang J · 2026 Apr 17
Study Type: Retrospective cohort study
Key Question: Does hydroxychloroquine initiation in adults with isolated discoid lupus erythematosus reduce 5-year cardiovascular and cardiometabolic risk?
Key Findings:
  • Single-center cohort (106 patients): HCQ use associated with significantly lower odds of hyperlipidaemia, peripheral arterial disease, angina, and coronary artery disease
  • Multi-institutional matched cohort (2,260 pairs): HCQ use associated with significantly reduced risk of hypertension, hyperlipidaemia, diabetes, coronary artery disease, and stroke
  • Benefits observed regardless of DLE disease extent
Clinical Relevance: This suggests early HCQ initiation in isolated DLE patients may provide cardiovascular protection beyond skin disease control, potentially informing treatment decisions in UK dermatology practice where cardiovascular comorbidity management is increasingly recognised as important.
Limitations: Retrospective design with potential residual confounding and reliance on diagnostic coding without direct assessment of disease activity.
PubMed →DOI →
Journal of the American Academy of Dermatology

Plastic in Dermatology: Impact on human health, skin disease and the contribution of personal care product use to environmental pollution

Anderson A, Tan E, Dunlop S · 2026 Apr 20
Study Type: Narrative review/commentary
Key Question: What are the health and environmental impacts of plastic components in personal care products used in dermatology practice?
Key Findings:
  • Identifies three main sources of plastic exposure from personal care products: intentionally added microbeads, micro/nanoplastics shed from packaging degradation, and plastic-associated chemicals
  • Reviews evidence linking personal care product plastic components to both human health concerns and environmental pollution
  • Proposes recommendations for reducing plastic-related impacts from dermatological personal care products
Clinical Relevance: UK dermatologists should consider the environmental and health implications when recommending personal care products to patients, particularly given increasing patient awareness and NHS sustainability commitments.
Limitations: Abstract provides insufficient detail about the quality or quantity of evidence reviewed, making it difficult to assess the strength of conclusions drawn.
PubMed →DOI →
Journal of the European Academy of Dermatology and Venereology : JEADV

Systemic effects of sunlight: 10-year review of cardiovascular, infection and cancer outcomes

Ziglar JA, Quiñonez RL, Mpyisi LFM et al. · 2026 Apr 20
Study Type: Narrative review
Key Question: What are the systemic health effects of sunlight and UV radiation exposure on cardiovascular disease, infections, cancer, and mortality outcomes?
Key Findings:
  • Most of 28 studies (2015-2025) showed inverse associations between UV exposure and adverse health outcomes, suggesting protective effects
  • Large cohort study found 2000 kJ/m² higher annual solar radiation exposure associated with 12% lower all-cause mortality risk and average 26 fewer days of life lost over 15.7 years
  • Studies covered cardiovascular outcomes (n=4), infectious diseases (n=12), malignancies (n=6), and all-cause mortality (n=6)
Clinical Relevance: Challenges dermatologists to consider balanced counselling about sun exposure, acknowledging potential systemic benefits alongside established skin cancer risks when advising patients about photoprotection strategies.
Limitations: Significant confounding by healthy user effect and socioeconomic factors limits causal interpretation of observed associations.
PubMed →DOI →
Journal of the European Academy of Dermatology and Venereology : JEADV

Practical recommendations for seborrheic dermatitis management: An expert consensus

Naldi L, Kyrgidis A, Tiplica GS et al. · 2026 Apr 21
Study Type: Expert consensus using modified Delphi methodology
Key Question: What are the evidence-based practical recommendations for managing seborrhoeic dermatitis in both acute and maintenance phases?
Key Findings:
  • 33 European dermatology experts achieved consensus on 44 of 68 refined clinical statements (65% agreement rate) after two voting rounds
  • Combined antifungal and anti-inflammatory therapy recommended as first-line for both acute flares and long-term maintenance
  • Structured therapeutic escalation pathway to systemic agents established for extensive or treatment-resistant cases
Clinical Relevance: Provides UK dermatologists with harmonised European expert guidance for seborrhoeic dermatitis management, particularly valuable where formal NICE or BAD guidelines may be limited.
Limitations: Consensus reflects expert opinion rather than new primary research evidence, with 35% of proposed statements failing to achieve agreement.
PubMed →DOI →
Journal of the European Academy of Dermatology and Venereology : JEADV

International consensus on dose reduction of biologics for patients with psoriasis: The DR. Delphi study

van Riel CAM, Boehncke WH, Lambert JLW et al. · 2026 Apr 21
Study Type: International modified eDelphi consensus study
Key Question: When and how should dermatologists reduce biologic doses in adult patients with psoriasis?
Key Findings:
  • 62 international dermatologists achieved consensus (≥70% agreement, <15% disagreement) on 11 statements across two rounds
  • Consensus reached on specific criteria for initiating, continuing, and discontinuing dose reduction, plus a two-step dose reduction algorithm for adalimumab, etanercept, and ustekinumab
  • IL-17 and IL-23 inhibitor dose reduction was deemed feasible but acknowledged to have limited supporting evidence
Clinical Relevance: Provides the first international guidance for biologic dose reduction in psoriasis, addressing a common but previously unguided practice that could reduce treatment costs and adverse effects in the NHS.
Limitations: Consensus based on expert opinion rather than robust clinical trial evidence, particularly for newer biologics.
PubMed →DOI →
The British journal of dermatology

Proliferation-associated protein 2G4 promotes keratinocyte proliferation and survival in psoriasis

Raunegger T, Wasserer S, Eigemann J et al. · 2026 Apr 20
Study Type: Experimental study combining transcriptomic analysis and functional studies
Key Question: Does proliferation-associated protein 2G4 (PA2G4) contribute to keratinocyte hyperproliferation in psoriasis and represent a potential therapeutic target?
Key Findings:
  • PA2G4 expression was highly abundant in psoriatic skin, specifically in basal proliferating keratinocytes, and correlated positively with disease severity and acanthosis
  • CRISPR/Cas9 knockout of PA2G4 in primary human keratinocytes reduced proliferation, activated differentiation pathways, and promoted cell death
  • Pharmacological inhibition with small-molecule WS6 similarly downregulated proliferation and survival genes, suggesting therapeutic potential
Clinical Relevance: PA2G4 inhibition could offer a novel therapeutic approach for psoriasis patients, particularly those with suboptimal responses to current treatments—relevant given NHS initiatives to improve long-term psoriasis management.
Limitations: This is primarily laboratory-based research without clinical trial data demonstrating efficacy or safety in human patients.
PubMed →DOI →
